tiers.tier_1_easy: Conceptual or single-script. Tests fundamental knowledge and skill triggering. No evasion, no multi-tool orchestration.
tiers.tier_2_medium: 2-3 scripts in sequence. Core WAF workflows. Script invocation validated in assertions.
tiers.tier_3_hard: Script-only solvable for at least one assertion. Requires evasion awareness or CVE-specific knowledge. Some assertions CANNOT pass without running actual scripts.
tiers.tier_4_expert: Full pipeline with combined tools, MCP awareness, and orchestration across multiple subsystems.

eval_design.mcp_tracking: Assertions for evals 7-9 check whether CRS Sandbox (chrome-devtools MCP) or Context7 documentation lookup was referenced.
eval_design.script_only_evals: Evals 4, 5, 7, 8, 9 have assertions that can ONLY pass if the actual skill script is invoked — not reimplemented.

evals.name: paranoia-level-recommendation
evals.prompt: We're setting up OWASP CRS v4 for the first time on our new platform. It's an API-only SaaS product — all traffic is machine-to-machine JSON REST, no HTML forms, no browser users at all, and no user-generated content. We have 60 enterprise B2B customers and a 99.99% uptime SLA. What paranoia level should we start at, and what's the recommended upgrade path if we want to get to PL2 or PL3 eventually?
evals.expected_output: Concrete PL recommendation (PL1 or PL2 for API-only, not PL3/PL4 initially) with explanation, phased upgrade plan, mention of detection-only/sampling mode, and reference to CRS application profiles or API-specific guidance.
assertions: Recommends PL1 or PL2 as the starting point — not PL3 or PL4 — with a clear explanation of why higher PLs would generate unacceptable false positives for an API-only platform
Explains anomaly scoring and threshold tuning (e.g., threshold of 5 for PL1) in the context of machine-to-machine traffic
Mentions detection-only mode or sampling mode as the recommended initial deployment approach before enabling blocking
Provides a phased upgrade path (e.g., PL1 → tune FPs → PL2 → tune) with milestones or criteria for moving up
References API application profiles, CRS application profile documentation, or explicitly acknowledges that API-only deployments have different false positive profiles than browser-facing apps

evals.name: block-bad-cidr
evals.prompt: We're under a brute-force attack on our login endpoint. All the attacks are coming from the CIDR range 185.220.101.0/24, which is a known Tor exit node range. I need a custom ModSecurity rule that blocks any request from this IP range to POST /auth/login, returns 403, and logs it. Run validate_rule.py to check it. Don't affect traffic to any other endpoint or from any other IP range.
evals.expected_output: SecRule using @ipMatch or @ipMatchFromFile scoped to POST /auth/login, deny + status:403 action, ID in 100000-199999 range, validated with validate_rule.py showing no errors.
assertions: Rule uses @ipMatch or @ipMatchFromFile operator with the 185.220.101.0/24 CIDR range
Rule is scoped to POST /auth/login (uses REQUEST_URI and REQUEST_METHOD conditions or a chain) and does NOT block all endpoints globally
Rule ID falls in the custom range 100000-199999
validate_rule.py is run on the rule file and confirms no syntax errors
Rule includes deny, status:403, and a log or logdata action for audit trail

evals.name: path-traversal-custom-rule
evals.prompt: I'm running ModSecurity v3 with CRS v4 at PL1 on our e-commerce app. We're getting path traversal probes hitting our image endpoint: GET /api/images/../../etc/passwd and GET /api/images/%2e%2e%2fconfig.yml. I need a custom rule that catches path traversal specifically on the /api/images/ path, blocks with 403, and doesn't interfere with legit requests like GET /api/images/product-123.jpg. Write me the rule, run validate_rule.py to validate it, run lint_regex.py to check for ReDoS risks, and use generate_ftw_test.py to scaffold the go-ftw test with both attack and benign cases.
evals.expected_output: SecRule in 100000-199999 range targeting REQUEST_URI for path traversal including URL-decoded variants, validated with validate_rule.py (no errors), linted with lint_regex.py (no ReDoS), go-ftw YAML (from generate_ftw_test.py) with at least one attack case (403) and one benign case (200).
assertions: Rule ID falls in the custom range 100000-199999
validate_rule.py is run on the rule file and confirms no syntax errors
lint_regex.py is run and confirms no ReDoS vulnerabilities in the regex pattern
generate_ftw_test.py is invoked to generate the go-ftw YAML test skeleton, with at least one attack case (expects 403) and one benign case (expects 200)
Rule detects both unencoded path traversal (../) and URL-encoded variants (%2e%2e%2f or %252e%252e%252f)

evals.name: fp-search-scope-out
evals.prompt: Our WAF (ModSecurity v3 + CRS v4 at PL2) is generating false positives on our e-commerce search endpoint. Rule 941130 fires when users search for legitimate terms like 'select dropdown', 'table reservation', or 'frame design'. These are real searches hitting ARGS:q on GET /api/search. We cannot disable 941130 globally or drop to PL1. Use generate_exclusion.py to produce the most precisely scoped exclusion possible, validate it with validate_rule.py, and explain why your scoping choices protect real XSS detection while eliminating the FP.
evals.expected_output: generate_exclusion.py invoked with --rule-id 941130 --uri /api/search --param ARGS:q --type runtime. validate_rule.py confirms no errors. Explanation of why ARGS:q scoping is safer than global rule removal.
assertions: generate_exclusion.py is explicitly invoked with --rule-id 941130 and --param ARGS:q (scoped to the parameter, not global rule removal)
The exclusion uses --type runtime (ctl: approach) so it goes BEFORE the CRS include directive — configure-time SecRuleRemoveById is not used as the primary solution
The exclusion is scoped to the /api/search URI using the --uri flag — not a global exclusion
validate_rule.py is run on the generated exclusion file and confirms no syntax errors
The response explains why scoping to ARGS:q preserves real XSS detection on other variables like REQUEST_BODY and REQUEST_HEADERS

evals.name: graphql-fp-runtime-exclusion
evals.prompt: Our WAF (ModSecurity v3 + CRS v4 at PL2) is blocking legitimate POST requests to /api/graphql with rule 942100 (SQL Injection). These are real GraphQL mutations from our React frontend using Apollo Client. A sample blocked request body looks like: {"query":"mutation UpdateOrder { updateOrder(id: 456, note: \"Transfer approved for account ref: OR 1=1\") { id status } }"}. We don't want to disable 942100 globally or drop to PL1. Use generate_exclusion.py to tune this false positive out, then validate the result.
evals.expected_output: generate_exclusion.py invoked with --rule-id 942100 --uri /api/graphql --type runtime producing a ctl:ruleRemoveById=942100 exclusion. Validated with validate_rule.py. Load-order explanation (before CRS include). Warning against global disable.
assertions: generate_exclusion.py is explicitly invoked with --rule-id 942100 and --uri /api/graphql
The generated exclusion uses the runtime (ctl:) approach, placed BEFORE the CRS include directive
The exclusion rule ID is in the 100000-199999 custom range
validate_rule.py is run on the generated exclusion and shows no syntax errors
The response warns against disabling 942100 globally or dropping the paranoia level

evals.name: log-triage-workflow
evals.prompt: I just pulled our weekly ModSecurity audit log and ran analyze_log.py --top-rules 10. Output: rule 942100 fired 1,247 times, rule 941170 fired 389 times, rule 949110 (blocking rule) fired 23 times. This is a SaaS B2B app with 40 enterprise customers, running PL1, anomaly threshold 5. Walk me through: (1) the right investigation order, (2) how to use analyze_log.py --rule-id --detail to distinguish real attacks from false positives, (3) how to handle confirmed FPs using generate_exclusion.py, and (4) how to verify real attack payloads with the CRS Sandbox.
evals.expected_output: Triage that prioritizes 949110 first, uses analyze_log.py --rule-id --detail per rule, explains TP vs FP signals (matched variable, IP diversity, payload repetition), invokes generate_exclusion.py for confirmed FPs, and references CRS Sandbox for attack payload verification. Maps to OODA loop.
assertions: 949110 is correctly identified as highest priority because it is the blocking rule — every fire is a real blocked customer request — and must be investigated before the higher-volume detection rules
analyze_log.py --rule-id <id> --detail is explicitly recommended or shown for at least two of the three rules
TP vs FP classification signals are explained: matched variable context, IP diversity, and payload repetition patterns
generate_exclusion.py is recommended or invoked for confirmed false positives, with a note on placement before the CRS include
CRS Sandbox (sandbox.coreruleset.org) is referenced as the verification tool for suspicious payloads

evals.name: cve-2025-29927-virtual-patch
evals.prompt:
CVE-2025-29927 is a critical Next.js middleware authorization bypass (CVSS 9.1, affects versions 14.x < 14.2.25, 15.x < 15.2.3). Attackers inject the header 'x-middleware-subrequest: middleware' to skip all authentication middleware. A recursion bypass also exists: 'x-middleware-subrequest: middleware:middleware:middleware:middleware:middleware'. Your Next.js 14.x app runs an unpatched version and the fix won't be deployed until next week.

You need an emergency virtual patch. Use bash scripts/new_incident.sh CVE-2025-29927 to scaffold the incident workspace. Write rules.conf with a blocking rule that covers: (1) the basic payload, (2) the recursion bypass, (3) case-insensitive header name detection. Write go-ftw regression tests in incidents/CVE-2025-29927/tests/. Assemble with bash scripts/assemble_rules.sh CVE-2025-29927. Validate with validate_rule.py. Use Context7 MCP or documentation to confirm CVE details before writing the rule.
evals.expected_output: bash scripts/new_incident.sh CVE-2025-29927 scaffolds workspace and prints allocated rule ID block. rules.conf has a SecRule with t:lowercase detecting x-middleware-subrequest header covering both basic and recursion variants. go-ftw tests cover attack (basic), attack (recursion), and benign (no header). assemble_rules.sh assembles to custom-rules.conf. validate_rule.py confirms no errors. Context7 or documentation referenced.
assertions: bash scripts/new_incident.sh CVE-2025-29927 is explicitly invoked to create the incident workspace — the allocated rule ID block printed by the script is used for the rules (this assertion FAILS if the incident directory is created manually without running the script)
The rule in rules.conf uses t:lowercase transform or REQUEST_HEADERS_NAMES to detect x-middleware-subrequest case-insensitively, catching both 'x-middleware-subrequest' and 'X-Middleware-Subrequest'

The rule handles both the basic payload ('middleware') and the colon-recursion bypass ('middleware:middleware:middleware:...') — either via @rx pattern that matches any occurrence of 'middleware' in the header value, or via @pm with both patterns
bash scripts/assemble_rules.sh CVE-2025-29927 is explicitly invoked to assemble rules into custom-rules.conf
validate_rule.py is run on the rules.conf and confirms valid syntax — MCP documentation (Context7) or a web lookup of CVE-2025-29927 details is referenced before writing the rule

evals.name: evasion-bypass-analysis
evals.prompt:
We deployed a WAF rule to block CVE-2025-29927 (Next.js middleware bypass via x-middleware-subrequest header). Our pentest team found three bypasses:

(1) Mixed case: 'X-MIDDLEWARE-SUBREQUEST: middleware' — our rule only checks lowercase header name
(2) Colon-recursion: 'x-middleware-subrequest: middleware:src/pages/api/auth:middleware:middleware' — our rule only matches the exact string 'middleware'
(3) URL-encoded value: 'x-middleware-subrequest: %6d%69%64%64%6c%65%77%61%72%65' (URL-encoding of 'middleware') — our rule doesn't decode before matching

Current broken rule:
SecRule REQUEST_HEADERS:x-middleware-subrequest '@pm middleware' 'id:100300,phase:1,deny,status:403,log,msg:"CVE-2025-29927"'

Explain precisely why each bypass works against this rule. Write a hardened replacement that closes all three gaps. Run lint_regex.py on any new regex pattern and validate_rule.py on the final rule.
evals.expected_output: Analysis: (1) REQUEST_HEADERS:x-middleware-subrequest is case-insensitive for the variable but header values aren't lowercased — t:lowercase needed; (2) @pm 'middleware' won't match 'middleware:path:...' — need @rx or @contains for any occurrence; (3) URL-encoded value bypasses @pm 'middleware' without t:urlDecodeUni. Hardened rule uses multiple transforms. lint_regex.py and validate_rule.py both run on the fix.
assertions: Correctly identifies that bypass #1 (case) requires adding t:lowercase transform OR using REQUEST_HEADERS_NAMES to normalize header name detection

Correctly identifies that bypass #2 (colon-recursion) requires matching 'middleware' anywhere in the header value, not as an exact string — e.g., switching from '@pm middleware' to '@rx middleware' or '@contains middleware' after applying t:lowercase
Correctly identifies that bypass #3 (URL encoding) requires adding t:urlDecodeUni (or t:urldecode) transform to decode the value before the @pm/@rx match
lint_regex.py is explicitly run on any regex pattern in the hardened rule to verify no ReDoS risk
validate_rule.py is explicitly run on the final hardened rule and confirms no syntax errors — rule ID is in the 100000-199999 range

evals.name: openapi-to-waf-pipeline
evals.prompt: We have a payment API and want positive-security WAF rules that allow only what the OpenAPI spec permits. The spec is at evals/files/payment-api.yaml (relative to the skill root). Generate rules using python scripts/openapi_to_rules.py, validate with validate_rule.py, check CRS convention compliance with lint_crs_rule.py, and generate a go-ftw test suite with generate_ftw_test.py. The rules must enforce: (1) only /api/payments and /api/payments/{id} are allowed paths — other paths return 403, (2) POST /api/payments requires Content-Type: application/json, (3) POST and DELETE to /api/payments/{id} require an Authorization header. Use Context7 MCP to look up current openapi_to_rules.py documentation if needed. After generating, verify via CRS Sandbox that GET /api/admin/config returns 403. Document everything in REPORT.md.
evals.expected_output: openapi_to_rules.py run on payment-api.yaml generating allowed-path, content-type, and auth header rules. validate_rule.py and lint_crs_rule.py confirm no errors. generate_ftw_test.py produces test stubs with allowed and blocked test cases. CRS Sandbox referenced for verification. REPORT.md documents the workflow.
assertions: python scripts/openapi_to_rules.py is explicitly invoked on the payment-api.yaml spec file — the generated .conf output includes allowed-path enforcement and at least one content-type or auth enforcement rule (FAILS if rules are hand-written without running the script)
validate_rule.py is run on the generated .conf file and confirms no syntax errors across all generated rules
Generated rules enforce that POST /api/payments requires Content-Type: application/json — requests with wrong or missing Content-Type to this endpoint should be blocked
Generated rules enforce that POST and DELETE endpoints require an Authorization header — missing Authorization returns 403
generate_ftw_test.py is used to create a go-ftw test file, and CRS Sandbox (sandbox.coreruleset.org) is referenced as the verification step for the generated allowlist
